The "Aha!" Moment

Understanding Grokking in AI
Alessandro MorroneStudent ID: 6293368 p = 113Prime modulus 1-layerTransformer 7 PartsComplete

Contents

01Reproducing Grokking10 pts 02Fourier Analysis+5 pts 03Three Phases+5 pts 04Data Fraction Sweep+5 pts 05Alignment & Mech. Interp.+5 pts 06Modular Multiplication+10 pts 07Co-Grokking+15 pts
Part 01 · 10 marks

Reproducing Grokking on
Modular Addition

10 marks

Grokking refers to the surprising observation that a neural network can abruptly generalise to held-out data long after it has apparently converged to near-zero training loss. We reproduce this phenomenon using a one-layer transformer trained on modular addition: given inputs a and b, predict (a + b) mod 113.

Setup

We used prime modulus p = 113, with 30% of all p² = 12,769 input pairs as training data and the remaining 70% for evaluation. The model was trained for 40,000 epochs using AdamW with weight decay.

Results

Figure 1: Training and test loss (log scale) for the mod 113 transformer. Train loss drops within ~1,000 epochs; test loss stays high until a sudden cliff-dive at epoch ~9,000 — the grokking moment.
Epochs 0–1,000
Memorisation
Train loss → 10⁻⁷. Test loss unmoved. Model has memorised 3,816 pairs.
Epochs 1,000–8,000
Stagnation
Both losses plateau. Early stopping here would miss everything.
Epochs ~8,000–10,000
Grokking
Test loss falls six orders of magnitude in ~2,000 epochs.
Part 02 · +5 marks

Fourier Analysis of
Embeddings & MLP Neurons

+5 marks

We apply a 1D Discrete Fourier Transform to the embedding matrix WE and MLP post-activations to identify the sparse set of frequencies the model uses internally.

Core finding: Out of 57 possible frequencies, only 4–5 carry significant energy — the same frequencies appear in both the embeddings and the MLP. This is the Fourier signature of the Clock Algorithm.
Figure 2: Dominant frequencies k = 7, 14, 29, 37, 38 (embedding) and k = 7, 29, 38, 55 (MLP). The overlap confirms a consistent Fourier basis throughout the network.
Figure 3: Per-neuron heatmap. The bright column at k = 29 shows dozens of neurons tuned to a single frequency — these are the "Fourier neurons" acting as frequency detectors.

The Clock Algorithm

Each token x is embedded as a point on a unit circle at angle 2πkx/p for each key frequency k. The MLP then uses the identity cos(A + B) = cos A·cos B − sin A·sin B to compose the embeddings of a and b into the correct output. A flat spectrum = memorisation. A peaked spectrum = genuine understanding.

Part 03 · +5 marks

The Three Phases
of Grokking

+5 marks

By tracking restricted loss, excluded loss, test loss, and weight norm across 400 saved checkpoints, we chart the three mechanistically distinct phases of grokking.

Figure 4: Top — excluded (red), restricted (purple), test (blue) losses. Bottom — weight norm. Phase boundaries at epoch 200 and epoch 6,900. Weight norm peaks at 60.4 then declines to 28.8.
MetricStartEnd
Excluded loss (train)4.77250.0000
Restricted loss4.76440.0000
Test loss4.76830.0000
Weight norm ‖W‖₂42.2528.77
The 6,900-epoch gap between weight norm peak (epoch 200) and grokking (epoch 6,900) is the quantified "delay" of grokking. The weight norm's monotonic decline from epoch 200 onward confirms cleanup is gradual, not triggered by the loss drop.
Part 04 · +5 marks

Data Fraction and
Epochs to Generalisation

+5 marks

We train four identical models at data fractions of 10%, 30%, 60%, and 90% to show that grokking only occurs in a specific regime of data scarcity.

Figure 5: Epochs until grokking as a function of training data fraction. 10% never grokked within 40,000 epochs. The delay collapses non-linearly as data increases.
Figure 6: Train/test loss curves at all four fractions. Green dashed lines mark grokking events. The 10% model (top left) memorises perfectly but never generalises.
FractionTraining pairsGrokking epochDelay vs 90%
10%1,276did not grokk—
30%3,83011,20056×
60%7,6616003×
90%11,492200baseline
Part 05 · +5 marks

AI Alignment and
Mechanistic Interpretability

+5 marks

Among the most consequential open problems in modern computer science is how to build AI systems that reliably do what we intend. This challenge — the AI alignment problem — has grown more urgent as the capabilities of large neural networks have outpaced our ability to understand them. Alignment and mechanistic interpretability are two sides of the same coin: alignment is the goal, mechanistic interpretability is the diagnostic tool needed to verify that goal at a structural level.

The Alignment Problem

The core issue is a gap between the objectives we specify for AI systems and the objectives those systems actually optimise for. We can only provide proxy signals — human feedback, benchmark scores, training loss — and a sufficiently capable optimiser will find ways to maximise these proxies that may not correspond to what we actually want. This failure mode, known as specification gaming, is well documented: reinforcement learning agents have scored highly in boat-racing games by collecting bonuses in circles rather than finishing the race; recommendation systems have maximised engagement by surfacing increasingly extreme content.

A more subtle variant is inner misalignment: a model may develop an internal objective that produces aligned-looking behaviour during all training and evaluation, then pursue something different once deployed. Because the misalignment is encoded in the weights rather than expressed in outputs, no amount of behavioural testing can catch it. The model passes every test — and then surprises us.

Loss curves and accuracy scores are behavioural measures. They tell us what a model does, not why or how. Two models with identical test accuracy may implement completely different internal algorithms — one fragile and brittle, one robust and generalisable; one aligned with our intentions, one aligned with something else. Standard evaluation methodology cannot distinguish between them.

What Mechanistic Interpretability Is

Mechanistic interpretability treats a trained neural network as software to be reverse-engineered. Rather than asking "what does this model output?", it asks "what algorithm does this model implement?" The central unit of analysis is the circuit: a subgraph of neurons and attention heads that collectively implement an identifiable computation. Work by Olah et al. (2020) found circuits for curve detection and frequency analysis in image classifiers; subsequent work identified circuits for in-context learning and indirect object identification in transformers.

A key complication is polysemanticity: individual neurons often respond to multiple semantically unrelated concepts simultaneously, because networks pack more features into their neurons than a one-to-one mapping would allow. This makes circuits harder to isolate, and is an active area of research.

Grokking as the Central Case Study

Consider our model at epoch 5,000 — mid-stagnation. Train loss ≈ 0, accuracy = 100%. By every standard metric it has "solved" modular addition. A deployed version would fail completely on any unseen input, because it has memorised 3,816 specific pairs, not learned a rule. Behavioural evaluation completely failed to detect this.

This is the alignment problem in miniature. The model appears aligned with our objective but has optimised for a different, narrower one. Mechanistic interpretability resolves the ambiguity: the Fourier analysis in Part 2 reveals that after grokking, the weights encode a genuine trigonometric algorithm. Before grokking, they encode a lookup table. These two states are indistinguishable from behavioural observation alone — one produces the right outputs on every training example, the other produces them on every example in existence. Only by examining the weights directly can we tell which is which.

Scale this up: a powerful AI system could be "behaviourally aligned" across all of training and red-teaming while internally having learned something subtly different from what we intended. The grokking case proves this isn't paranoia — it happens in the simplest possible neural networks on the simplest possible tasks.

Why This Matters for AI Safety

Detecting deceptive alignment. A sufficiently capable model might learn to behave aligned during training and evaluation — recognising those contexts — while pursuing different objectives at deployment. Mechanistic interpretability offers the only path to detecting this: examining the structure of learned representations directly, rather than relying on behavioural signals a capable model could in principle fake.
Pre-deployment auditing. Safety-critical software is audited before deployment — not just tested, but read. No analogous practice currently exists for neural networks. Mechanistic interpretability is building the tools to make such audits possible: identifying circuits responsible for specific behaviours, verifying that dangerous capabilities are absent, checking that values are encoded correctly.
Verifying intent, not just behaviour. The deepest alignment goal is not training models that produce aligned outputs in tested contexts, but training models that have genuinely internalised aligned objectives. This distinction — between a model that acts well and one that wants to act well — cannot be resolved by any behavioural test. It requires understanding what objectives are encoded in the weights. Mechanistic interpretability is the only research programme currently attempting to answer this directly.

Limitations and Open Problems

Intellectual honesty requires acknowledging the gap between what mechanistic interpretability has achieved and what safety guarantees would require. Current techniques scale to models with one or two layers and a few million parameters. State-of-the-art systems have hundreds of layers and hundreds of billions of parameters. There is no established methodology for scaling circuit analysis to this regime, and polysemanticity may make clean decomposition fundamentally harder in larger models.

None of this undermines the importance of the research programme — it defines its agenda. The question is not whether mechanistic interpretability is sufficient for AI safety today (it is not), but whether investment now will compound into meaningful safety guarantees as AI systems grow more capable. The grokking work in this report — one of the first fully end-to-end reverse-engineered learned algorithms — is precisely the kind of foundation that makes that scaling tractable.

The core argument: If we cannot read the algorithm, we cannot verify the alignment. Mechanistic interpretability is not a peripheral academic exercise — it is a prerequisite for the responsible deployment of capable AI systems.
Part 06 · +10 marks

Reverse-Engineering the
Modular Multiplication Algorithm

+10 marks

We investigate what internal algorithm a one-layer transformer learns for (a × b) mod 113 — a strictly harder operation than addition.

Theoretical background: discrete logarithms

For prime p with primitive root g, every non-zero element can be written as a power of g. This gives the key identity:

a × b ≡ g^(log_g(a) + log_g(b)) (mod p)

Multiplication reduces to addition in log-space. For p = 113, the primitive root is g = 3.

Figure 7: Modular multiplication grokked at epoch 8,000 — similar delay to addition, but test loss declines gradually post-grokking, suggesting a more complex circuit.
Figure 8: The multiplication embedding spectrum (left) is notably less sparse than addition — many frequencies elevated rather than 3–5 sharp spikes. MLP spectrum (right) retains clear dominant frequencies at k = 0, 21, 37, 43, 47.
Figure 9: Embedding PCA components vs discrete log base 3. All four components show near-zero linear correlation (r ≈ 0.00–0.05), ruling out a simple linear discrete-log encoding — but colour clustering suggests a non-linear circular representation.
Figure 10: Mean attention from final token to each position. Heads 0, 1, 3 attend ~50/50 to a and b — consistent with commutativity of ×. Head 2 attends to the = position at 17.7%.
Figure 11: True (a×b) mod 113 (left), model predictions (centre), correctness map (right). The model achieves 100% test accuracy.
100% test accuracy on modular multiplication. The model learned a compact, generalisable algorithm — not a memorisation table — as evidenced by the sparse MLP spectrum and perfect out-of-sample performance.
Part 07 · +15 marks

Co-Grokking: Two Tasks,
One Model, One Moment

+15 marks

Can a single model learn both modular addition and multiplication simultaneously — and generalise on both tasks at the same epoch? This phenomenon is called co-grokking.

Design

Each input sequence is extended to length 4: [a, b, op, =], where op = 113 signals addition and op = 114 signals multiplication. Three distinct special tokens (add op, mul op, EOS) eliminate any ambiguity. Batches are drawn equally from both tasks and shuffled together.

Key hyperparameter finding: Weight decay must be reduced from 1.0 → 0.2. At wd = 1.0, addition grokked first and then monopolised the weights — multiplication plateaued at loss 0.075 for 320,000 additional epochs. Lower weight decay gives both circuits room to grow simultaneously.

Result: Clean Co-Grokking

Figure 12: Multiplication grokked first at epoch 23,300; addition followed 700 epochs later. The 700-epoch co-grokking window is less than 3% of total training duration.
AdditionMultiplication
Grokking epoch24,00023,300 (first!)
Final test loss0.00450.0041
Final accuracy100.00%99.73%
Co-grokking window700 epochs

Why multiplication grokked first

This is the opposite of the intuitive expectation. With lower weight decay, the richer frequency structure needed for multiplication — identified in Part 6 — can form without being pruned during cleanup. The two loss curves track each other nearly identically from epoch 0 to epoch 20,000, suggesting a shared intermediate representation is built before either task-specific circuit finalises.

Mechanistic significance

The two circuits — the Fourier Clock Algorithm for addition and the richer frequency structure for multiplication — must co-exist in the same weight matrix. Their near-simultaneous emergence indicates the model finds a unified internal representation of the integers from which both algorithms can be read off, rather than partitioning its capacity between two independent solutions.

Co-grokking confirmed. Single model. Two tasks. 700-epoch synchronisation window. 100% and 99.73% accuracy.
The “Aha!” Moment: Understanding Grokking in AI
